Notice Of Motion For Approval Of Dismissal Of Action Pursuant To Cal. Govt Code 12652(C)(1); Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Feb-27-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF KEN ELDER's Motion For Approval Of Dismissal Of Action Pursuant To Cal. Govt Code 12652(C)(1).

Qui Tam Plaintiff Ken Elder's unopposed Motion for Approval of Dismissal of Action Pursuant to California Government Code Section 12652(c)(1) is DENIED. This is a False Claims Act case in which qui tam plaintiff Elder is pursuing a case for himself and the State of California against Defendant JP Morgan Chase Bank, N.A. alleging violations of section 12651. A qui tam plaintiff's action may be dismissed after filing "only with the written consent of the court and the Attorney General or prosecuting authority of a political subdivision, or both, as appropriate under the allegations of the civil action, taking into account the best interests of the parties involved and the public purposes behind this act." (Gov. Code, section 12652(c)(1).) "No claim for any violation of Section 12651 may be waived or released by any private person, except if the action is part of a court approved settlement of a false claim civil action brought under this section." (Ibid.) Elder seeks to dismiss his and the State's claim with prejudice. For one thing, the Attorney General has not consented in writing. Moreover, the court does not find on this record, weighing the public purposes behind the FCA and the interest of the parties, including the People of the State of California, dismissal with prejudice is appropriate. Further, the requested dismissal with prejudice amounts to Elder releasing a claim for a violation of section 12651, but it is not part of a court approved settlement. Thus, dismissal, let alone dismissal with prejudice, on this record would be inconsistent with the express language of section 12652(c)(1).

Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling and drop the hard copy in Dept. 302's inbox.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

Off Calendar Jun-13-2023 Off Calendar

RELATOR'S APPLICATION FOR LEAVE TO FILE THIRD AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-13-2023 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. DAVID S. GOLUB, Esq. of Silver Golub & Teitell LLP (Tel: 203-325-4491) for Relator;
Atty. JENNIFER M. WOLLENBERG, Esq. of Morgan, Lewis & Bockius LLP (Tel: 202-739-3000) for defendant JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A.

HEARING RE: RELATOR'S APPLICATION FOR LEAVE TO FILE THIRD AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's "Application for Leave to File Third Amended Complaint" is granted. The motion is timely, there is a liberal policy in favor of amendment, and the proposed amendment does not prejudice defendant since there is no trial date and the amendment does not change the tenor of the case.

Judicial estoppel does not prevent plaintiff from seeking to plead a claim under sec. 1510(b)(1) of the Unclaimed Property Law (CCP 1500 et seq. ("UPL").)

The judicial estoppel "doctrine applies when '(1) the same party has taken two positions; (2) the positions were taken in judicial or quasi-judicial administrative proceedings; (3) the party was successful in asserting the first position (i.e., the tribunal adopted the position or accepted it as true); (4) the two positions are totally inconsistent; and (5) the first position was not taken as a result of ignorance, fraud, or mistake.'" (Aguilar v. Lerner (2004) 32 Cal.4th 974, 986-87.) Judicial estoppel is predicated on the principle that a party should not be allowed to "gain[] advantage by taking one position, and then seeking a second advantage by taking an incompatible position." (Aguilar v. Lerner (2004) 32 Cal.4th 974, 986.)

Here, plaintiff's notice of dismissal of the UPL sec. 1511 claim was not a promise or representation that it would not attempt to pursue a UPL sec. 1510(b)(1) claim. Indeed, the parties agreed that plaintiff's dismissal did not impact the issue presented in defendant's petition before the California Supreme Court, i.e., whether State Controller notice is a statutory prerequisite for penalties. (Wollenberg Dec., Ex. 1 at 7; Golub Dec., Ex. D at 1.) The court therefore cannot conclude that plaintiff took two inconsistent positions or that the California Supreme Court adopted plaintiff's first position as a basis to dismiss the petition for review.

Defendant argues that the UPL sec. 1510(b)(1) claim fails as a matter of law. Defendant may raise its merits arguments by subsequent motion. (Kittredge Sports Co. v. Superior Court (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 1045, 1048 ["[T]he preferable practice would be to permit the amendment and allow the parties to test its legal sufficiency"].)

Plaintiff must file a Third Amended Complaint. The court does not deem pleadings filed.

Order signed.

MINI MINUTES:
The following matters were heard concurrently:
CGC-19-579144 Ken Elder vs. J.P. Morgan Chase N.A. and
CGC-19-581373 Ken Elder vs. U.s. Bank N.A.

APPEARANCES:
David Golub, Esq. (203.325.4491 / Cell: 203.253.9444 / email: dgolub@sgtlaw.com)
of Silver Golub & Teitell LLP for Plaintiff/Relator, Ken Elder, via Zoom.

Douglas W. Baruch, Esq. (202.739.5219 / email: douglas.baruch@morganlewis.com) and Joseph E. Floren, Esq. (415.442.1391 / email: joseph.floren@morganlewis.com) of Morgan Lewis for Defendant, JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A.

A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT J.P. MORGAN CHASE, N.A.'s DEMURRER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT is taken under submission as of Nov. 1, 2021.
